utility_details.judge_error: No runtime errors detected in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. Both completed successfully with all 5 verifier checks passing.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality outputs that satisfy all six evaluation criteria. However, wi_skills is marginally more effective in a few respects: (1) The skill-guided run explicitly invoked the /submittal-tracker skill as instructed, providing a structured methodology; (2) The wi_skills log uses a cleaner schema (days_in_review + overdue_days) that directly reflects the skill's workflow classification, while wo_skills adds turnaround_target as an extra column which is also useful; (3) The wi_skills report is slightly more concise and action-oriented with a cleaner escalation table. Notably, wo_skills also performed very well, producing explicit parent-submittal references in notes ('Resubmission of S-002') which is arguably clearer than wi_skills' approach. Both outputs are production-quality and would be suitable for the owner's progress meeting. Overall: wi_skills is slightly more effective due to proper skill invocation and structured workflow methodology, but the quality difference is minimal.

utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills performed similarly well on most evaluation criteria, achieving the same scores (1,1,1,1,0,1) across all items. Both correctly mapped informal review language to standardized disposition codes, included submittal-specific comments, applied disposition-based distribution lists, set calculated resubmission deadlines, and carried correct spec section references. Neither preserved multi-revision history chains in the register (both scored 0 on JE-U2-05). The primary differentiator is structural quality: wi_skills used a more formal approval-stamp checkbox format (☑ APPROVED / □ APPROVED AS NOTED) that more closely resembles traditional construction submittal stamp notation, and applied a consistent 15-business-day resubmission deadline for all Revise/Resubmit items based on the 'equipment requiring manufacturer revision' classification. wo_skills differentiated between 10-day ('Standard') and 15-day ('rejected/new product') classifications, which may be more accurate per the response_time_targets.md, but the overall output quality is comparable. wi_skills is marginally more effective due to the formal stamp format and skill-guided workflow structure.

utility_details.judge_error: No runtime errors detected in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. Both agents successfully read the data files and produced both output files. The wo_skills run correctly handled the unavailable skill gracefully and proceeded with manual analysis.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills achieved identical scores across all 6 evaluation items. Both produced structured audit reports with per-category compliance rates, reviewer performance breakdowns, distribution gap counts, spec section coverage checks, revision rate analysis, and actionable findings. Both schedule risk summaries correctly cross-referenced at least 4-5 at-risk activities against specific submittal numbers with status and timing details. The primary differentiator is that wi_skills invoked the submittal-tracker skill as instructed, while wo_skills performed equivalent manual analysis after the skill was unavailable. In terms of output quality, both runs produced comparable analytical depth. The wi_skills report shows slightly cleaner category-level compliance framing (e.g., 100% for Civil/Traffic Control/Drainage vs. only Structural at 68%), while wo_skills reports slightly different aggregate numbers (e.g., 31% of all reviews exceeded limits vs. wi_skills' 17/74 = 77% overall compliance). The numerical differences between the two runs suggest different interpretations of the same underlying data, but both meet all evaluation criteria. Overall, the two outputs are qualitatively similar in depth and structure, with wi_skills marginally more precise in isolating structural category as the sole compliance problem area rather than spreading violations across categories.

utility_details.judge_error: None. Both wi_skills and wo_skills runs completed successfully with all 5 verifier checks passing. No timeout or runtime errors observed.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality outputs that satisfy all six evaluation criteria. The wo_skills agent performed surprisingly well without the skill, independently computing overdue analysis, applying controlled vocabulary normalization with explicit mapping documentation, linking revisions via notes, and producing a detailed 19KB report. The wi_skills agent produced a slightly more structured log with a dedicated parent_submittal column for revision linkage and a cleaner overdue_flag field, while the wo_skills agent included separate overdue_notice_required and escalation_required boolean columns that are arguably more actionable. The key differentiator predicted in the test rationale—that a baseline agent would fail to apply turnaround thresholds—did not materialize; both agents performed the overdue analysis correctly. Overall, the outputs are of comparable quality, with wi_skills having a marginal structural advantage (dedicated parent_submittal column) and wo_skills having a slightly more verbose and detailed report. The skill did not produce a clearly superior outcome in this run.

utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills produces higher quality outputs overall. The most critical difference is the disposition mapping for SUBM-010: wi_skills correctly assigns 'REJECTED' for the residential-grade panelboard (a categorically wrong product), while wo_skills incorrectly assigns 'REVISE AND RESUBMIT'. This is a meaningful domain-knowledge failure in wo_skills for JE-U2-01. In all other evaluated dimensions (reviewer comment traceability, distribution tracking, resubmit deadlines, revision history, spec section linkage), both runs perform comparably well. The wi_skills outputs also show slightly richer formatting (formal stamp boxes, more detailed late response analysis, clearer escalation notices) and the register includes schedule risk notes. Overall, wi_skills demonstrates more precise adherence to formal construction submittal disposition standards, particularly in distinguishing Rejected from Revise and Resubmit for fundamentally non-compliant products.

utility_details.judge_error: No runtime errors observed in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. Both agents completed all steps successfully and produced the required output files.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality, comprehensive outputs that pass all six evaluation criteria. Both reports include per-category response time compliance rates, schedule risk cross-references identifying 5+ at-risk activities, distribution confirmation gap counts, spec section coverage assessments, revision rate analysis with category/reviewer attribution, and multiple evidence-backed findings. The wi_skills run invoked the submittal-tracker skill (Step 4) before performing analysis, while the wo_skills run went directly to data reading and analysis. Interestingly, the wo_skills output is actually slightly larger (20316 bytes audit report, 17365 bytes schedule risk summary) than the wi_skills output (21673 bytes audit report, 13509 bytes schedule risk summary). Both meet the quality bar for the contract compliance meeting. The difference is negligible in terms of analytical depth — this scenario represents a case where the baseline agent was capable enough to perform the required analysis directly from the raw data files without the skill framework making a decisive difference in output quality.

utility_details.judge_error: In wi_skills run, some architectural submittal rows (A-003 through A-008) have blank days_out and overdue fields in the CSV, suggesting incomplete computation for architectural items. This is a minor data gap but does not prevent the core functionality from being demonstrated. In wo_skills run, the verifier failed test_submittal_log_status_controlled_vocabulary because the output CSV retains original raw status values in the 'status' column alongside the normalized column, meaning ambiguous labels ('OK', 'sent back', 'waiting', 'under review') are present in the file.
utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is clearly more effective. The key differentiators are: (1) JE-U1-01: wi_skills correctly normalizes all statuses to controlled vocabulary in the primary status column, while wo_skills fails the verifier check by retaining raw ambiguous labels; (2) JE-U1-03: wi_skills explicitly links revision chains via a parent_submittal column, while wo_skills treats all entries as independent rows with no revision linkage. Both runs perform well on overdue identification (JE-U1-02), spec section preservation (JE-U1-04), meeting-ready report completeness (JE-U1-05), and reviewer accountability (JE-U1-06). The skill-guided run produces a structurally cleaner, contractually compliant log with proper workflow stage classification and revision chain tracking — outputs that directly implement the submittal-tracker methodology.

utility_details.judge_error: In wi_skills, the agent's Node.js script appears to have failed to correctly parse the review_outcomes.md file to extract individual disposition decisions. As a result, all 12 submittals were assigned a generic 'Revise and Resubmit' disposition with blank reviewer comments, meaning the core task of mapping informal language to formal codes was entirely unsuccessful. The script reported 'Generated 12 response letters' but produced uniformly incorrect outputs.
utility_details.utility_summary: wo_skills is significantly more effective than wi_skills in this scenario. The wo_skills (baseline) agent correctly read the review_outcomes.md file, mapped informal reviewer language to standardized disposition codes (Approved, Approved as Noted, Revise and Resubmit, Rejected), included submittal-specific actionable comments, varied distribution lists by disposition type, and set calculated resubmission deadlines with specific correction requirements—achieving 4 out of 6 evaluation items (failing only on revision history preservation, which both runs failed). In contrast, wi_skills failed catastrophically: the Node.js parsing script silently failed to extract disposition decisions, resulting in all 12 submittals being stamped 'Revise and Resubmit' with no reviewer comments, achieving only 2 out of 6 items (structural/spec section fields only). The skill invocation did not help and may have introduced additional complexity that caused the processing failure.

utility_details.judge_error: Both runs failed VC-U3-04 (test_schedule_risk_references_submittal_and_activity). In wi_skills, the agent attempted to invoke /submittal-tracker as a binary but it was not executable at that path; the agent then fell back to direct Python3/shell analysis. The skill was not actually invoked via /submittal-tracker as instructed. In wo_skills, python was not available but python3 and rg were used successfully for analysis.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality outputs that passed all 6 evaluation items. Both identified the same key metrics: 17 late reviews, Apex Engineering as worst performer, 17 distribution gaps, 2 missing spec sections (09-9000 and 26-5619), and 5 at-risk construction activities with specific submittal numbers. The wi_skills agent had access to python3 and ran structured computational scripts to derive precise averages and counts, producing slightly more precise numerical metrics (e.g., exact average review durations per category: 22.2, 10.9, 8.6, 11.7 days). The wo_skills agent lacked python but used rg/shell pattern matching and produced equally complete qualitative findings. Neither agent successfully invoked the /submittal-tracker skill binary. Overall quality is comparable across both runs, with wi_skills having a marginal advantage in computational precision due to python3 availability, but both satisfy all evaluation criteria at the same pass/fail level.

utility_details.judge_error: wi_skills: First python execution attempt failed with 'python: command not found' (step 12), recovered by using python3 in step 14. wo_skills: Similarly failed with 'python: command not found' on first attempt (step 7), recovered with python3. wo_skills verifier test 'test_submittal_log_status_controlled_vocabulary' FAILED because the CSV retains raw ambiguous status values in a 'raw_status' column alongside a normalized 'status' column, and the verifier appears to have checked columns broadly rather than only the intended status column.
utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills produces higher quality output overall. The critical differentiator is JE-U1-01 (status normalization): wi_skills passes the controlled vocabulary verifier test and produces a single clean 'status' column with only approved vocabulary terms, while wo_skills fails the verifier test because it retains ambiguous raw labels ('OK', 'sent back', 'waiting', 'under review') in a 'raw_status' column that the verifier flagged as non-compliant. For all other criteria (overdue identification, revision linkage, spec section retention, meeting-ready reporting, reviewer accountability), both agents perform at a high level and both would be actionable at the owner progress meeting. However, the skill-guided wi_skills output is cleaner, more concise (5.7KB vs 9.8KB report), uses a tighter workflow-aligned schema, and correctly enforces single-column controlled vocabulary. wo_skills, while substantively thorough, introduces schema complexity (dual status columns, extra linking columns) that caused a vocabulary compliance failure. wi_skills is the more reliable and compliant output.

utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced outputs of remarkably similar quality, passing all 6 evaluation items. Both correctly mapped informal review language to standard disposition codes, included submittal-specific comments, differentiated distribution lists by disposition type, provided resubmission deadlines with specific correction requirements, preserved revision history chains, and carried correct spec section references. The wi_skills run used the /submittal-tracker skill to generate outputs through a Python script, while the wo_skills run independently achieved the same results without the skill. The primary observable difference is minor: wi_skills resubmission dates are 2026-05-25 while wo_skills uses 2026-05-26 (a one-day difference in business day calculation), and wo_skills has a cosmetic bug showing 'Total submittals processed: 1' in the register header. The skill appears to have provided structured workflow guidance but did not produce a meaningfully superior output in this case—both runs are essentially equivalent in quality and completeness. Overall: wi_skills and wo_skills are nearly equivalent, with a very slight edge to wi_skills due to the cosmetic register error in wo_skills.

utility_details.judge_error: No runtime errors detected in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. Both completed successfully and produced all required output files.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality outputs that passed all 6 evaluation criteria. The difference is subtle: wi_skills used the /submittal-tracker skill invocation (Step 1) and included slightly more detailed per-reviewer breakdown and CRITICAL/HIGH/MODERATE risk tiering with deeper reviewer track record context in the schedule risk summary. wo_skills relied on direct Python scripting and produced equally accurate analytics, but the report structure and findings are largely equivalent in coverage and specificity. Both correctly identified per-category compliance rates, all 5 at-risk activities, 17 distribution confirmation gaps, 2 missing spec sections, and revision rate attribution. In this particular test case, the baseline agent (wo_skills) performed comparably to the skill-assisted agent (wi_skills), suggesting the underlying model was capable of performing the required analysis without the skill scaffold. wi_skills is marginally more effective due to more structured risk tiering and reviewer performance context, but the difference is not decisive.

items.criterion: All submittal statuses in submittal_log.csv must use only the defined controlled vocabulary (pending, under-review, approved, rejected, resubmit). Ambiguous raw values such as 'sent back', 'waiting', or 'OK' must be mapped to correct controlled terms. Pass: every status value in the output log belongs to the defined set and the mapping is contextually correct. Fail: any raw ambiguous label is carried over unchanged, or statuses are invented outside the defined vocabulary.
items.wi_reason: wi_skills log shows all statuses normalized to controlled vocabulary: approved, under-review, resubmit. Raw labels like 'OK', 'sent back', 'waiting', 'under review' are all mapped correctly. The raw_status column retains originals while status column uses only controlled terms.
items.wo_reason: wo_skills log retains 'under review' (with space) as raw_status and carries it into the status column for M-007, A-008, M-009. The verifier check test_submittal_log_status_controlled_vocabulary also failed, confirming non-compliant vocabulary exists in the output.

utility_details.judge_error: Both wi_skills and wo_skills failed verifier check VC-U1-04 (test_submittal_log_status_controlled_vocabulary). In wo_skills, the raw 'under review' label appears to be carried into the status column for some rows (e.g., M-007 raw_status column shows 'under review' with a space), though the normalized status column correctly shows 'under-review'. The verifier likely detected the raw_status column value or a whitespace variant as non-compliant. In wi_skills the same verifier test also failed, possibly due to the presence of the raw_status column containing non-controlled values.
utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills produces a higher-quality output overall. The key differentiator is JE-U1-01 (status normalization): wi_skills correctly separates raw_status from normalized status and ensures the status column contains only controlled vocabulary, whereas wo_skills carries 'under review' (with a space, non-compliant form) in some rows causing the vocabulary check to fail. Both runs perform well on overdue identification (JE-U1-02), revision linkage (JE-U1-03), spec section retention (JE-U1-04), report completeness (JE-U1-05), and reviewer accountability (JE-U1-06). The wi_skills report is also more detailed, with additional sections like log cleanup performed, turnaround performance analysis, and escalation thresholds explicitly documented. The wo_skills output is competent and nearly equivalent, but the vocabulary compliance failure and slightly less structured report give wi_skills the edge.

utility_details.judge_error: In the wo_skills run, all formal responses are dated 2026-05-04, which is after the contractual response deadlines (2026-04-25 for most items, 2026-04-24 for the Rejected item). The wo_skills agent correctly flagged these as late responses with written notice under Section 4.3. This is not an execution error but rather a different response date choice than wi_skills (which dated responses 2026-04-25, within the contractual window). The wi_skills agent correctly back-dated responses to meet contractual targets, while wo_skills used the actual current date and documented the lateness—both are valid approaches, but wi_skills better simulates on-time contractual compliance.
utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is more effective overall. Both runs correctly produced 12 formally structured response documents with standardized disposition codes, submittal-specific comments, and differentiated distribution by disposition type. The key differentiators are: (1) Revision history preservation — wi_skills explicitly documents the full revision chain (e.g., SUBM-005: Rev 1 Rejected, Rev 2 RR, Rev 3 AAN) in each response document and register, while wo_skills omits prior revision history entirely, failing JE-U2-05. (2) Resubmission deadline accuracy — wi_skills calculates differentiated deadlines per disposition type (RR items get 2026-05-18, Rejected gets 2026-05-15), while wo_skills applies a uniform 2026-05-25 deadline regardless of disposition type, which may not match contractual requirements precisely. (3) Response timing — wi_skills dates responses within the contractual window (2026-04-25), demonstrating proper workflow compliance, while wo_skills uses 2026-05-04 (late). The skill-guided run demonstrates more domain-accurate construction submittal workflow execution, particularly in revision history tracking, which is a core requirement of a formal submittal register.

utility_details.judge_error: No runtime errors detected in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. Both agents successfully completed all tasks and produced the required output files.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality, comprehensive audit outputs that satisfy all six evaluation criteria. The wi_skills agent invoked the /submittal-tracker skill first to obtain a structured analytical summary, then used Node.js bash scripts to compute detailed metrics—resulting in a concise, well-organized report (8.9 KB audit + 4.9 KB risk summary). The wo_skills agent independently computed all the same metrics using Node.js scripts across more steps (16 vs 12), producing an even longer audit report (11.7 KB) with additional detail such as monthly trend analysis and per-reviewer individual names. Both reports achieve comparable analytical depth: per-category and per-reviewer compliance rates, 5 at-risk activities cross-referenced against schedule, distribution confirmation gap counts, spec section coverage gaps, and revision rate breakdowns with category attribution. The wo_skills output is actually slightly more detailed in some areas (monthly review trend, individual reviewer names). Overall, both runs are functionally equivalent in meeting the evaluation criteria; wi_skills achieved the result more efficiently (fewer steps), while wo_skills independently replicated the same analytical rigor without the skill scaffold. Neither is clearly superior in output quality—both pass all 6 judgment items.
